speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just-- just cut a minute. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. The date is January the 21st, 1997. The survivor is Teofil Modelski. And his name at birth was Modelski. The interviewer is Allan Kassell. And the interview is being done in the city of Huddersfield in England. And it's being conducted in English. The date is January 21st, 1997. I'm Alan Kassell, and I'm interviewing Teofil Modelski at Huddersfield in England, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you look at me, OK, when you're talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's good to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you're running are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Teofil Modelski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry about that. Fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, running. Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Teofil Modelski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same name was at birth that I've just said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-D-E-L-S-K-I. Model, Modelski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 28th of December, 1923, in Poznan Poland, Western side of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: At present, I've only just passed my birthday 20-- 73 years old. I begin 73 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your religion when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, religion, Roman Catholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your religion now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same as I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the town of Poznan in Poland, where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, Poznan was very industrial time-- town, with about 250,000 citizens before the war. I'm not sure how-- the amount reflects now. We lived in Poznan center, and then, during holiday time, we moved about 24 kilometers from Poznan to Brunow, where we had a summer holiday house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we talk about your home in the town? What sort of home was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a four-bedroom flat, where-- which we usually went winter time, otherwise I had to go to school in Poznan. And we were very comfortable. We had no television, at this time, neither-- only radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who, lived there with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my mother, my father, six-- five brothers, plus sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father was Zygmunt Modelski. My mother was Stanislawa Modelska.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your brothers called?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother was Zygmunt, then came Izydor, Mieczyslaw, which we called Michael, then Teofil, that's me, Jerzy, that's my twin brother, Barbara, my sister, and the youngest brother, Stanislaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the living accommodation like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very comfortable. And we lived very happily.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was ex-army major. He was retired, forced retirement for various political reasons, probably. And my mother was teacher from profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the political reasons you mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regarding my father? There was a change in Poland in 1926. The main leader of our country, Jozef Pilsudski, has made an outbreak and took dictator, in which he removed all the officers which were against his coup.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect do you remember that having on the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had a certain effect, but at that time, I was-- I was very young, so I didn't realize it. But later on, my father found himself a job in bank, where he worked. And we did see no change because he had his pension, plus whatever he earned in bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a good standard of living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good standard of living, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you had another home.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had another home, which was built from-- from beginning. It wasn't ready. We had-- our parents had to build it through-- through a construction company. And there we used to go every time holiday time. That means from June to-- to September. Which was very nice surrounding, around-- near the woodland and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do as a-- as a young boy? How did you spend your leisure time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My leisure time, I was-- I was still a student. I started going just before the war. I started going to grammar school. And the-- the usual boy fun. Through the forest, and playing with other boys of my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of a kid were you, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a rather naughty boy. We used to always have a fight with my twin brother, who always stayed with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were-- did you-- were you in any youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- I joined the scout movement, which was very good to me, because we had various trips with the scout movement. And we learned a lot of things to our benefit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things did you do in the scouts?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to do certain trips, where we played various games and so on. Therefore, with other boys of similar age, we managed to get together a lot better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your parents? What social circles did they move in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were their friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father was a army major, and therefore, we moved-- we didn't have many friends to come with, because we had a big family to move anywhere. It wasn't very easy. So once or twice a year, some friends came, a grammar school teacher, and so on, where my father-- he was my father's best friend. And that's the only time we've seen anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the family's relationship with the minorities in Poznan?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no differences. There were-- they felt like everybody else. My father even spoke to a German farmer who-- who lived next to where we had our holiday house. And he-- he liked to come and conversate with him, because he spoke German, and that way he could just remember his school days, when he was learning German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of a Jewish community in Poznan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there weren't many Jewish community in Western Poznan. There were more in the east of Poznan, but I wasn't aware of any at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't aware of anti-semitism or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had not heard of any anti-semitism. Although, I-- I read something in the paper once. But that was the-- through the German propaganda, which they tried to instill hatred in our country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things do you remember reading?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Something about the Jewish community being-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to buy various things for various reasons, and cheating the farmers, and so on-- which, in effect, I didn't realize, because I wasn't old enough to understand all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you at all aware of what was happening in Germany in the early 1930s?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't. I wasn't aware then. I was more aware near the outbreak of war. When the German propaganda used to shout that they want the passage through the corridor, Danzig, and so on. Then I realized, when the Polish army was mobilized and so on, that something is going to happen. Otherwise, I heard nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember your parents saying at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were very, very upset. And naturally, my father wanted to join the Polish army, as well, if necessary to fight for the country. And it was at that time, when the war broke out, a bit-- before the war broke out, just a few days, we moved-- I, my twin brother, moved to Warsaw in order to join the Polish forces. But the war was that quick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you on the first day of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the first day of the war, we were returning from our holiday house to Poznan. And then we'd seen aeroplanes flying over the-- over us, and we've seen Polish planes trying to knock them down. Then we realized the war is on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do, then, as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we moved to Poznan, because that was the first stage, when we seen aeroplanes going-- doing, you know, all these bombing and so on. That's the only thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you were coming back from your holiday home, were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were coming back to Poznan, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: From being on holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being on holiday, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- so when you got back to Poznan, then, with the German planes in the sky, what did you do as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed in our house. We lived on the fourth floor in the house. There was danger that any bombs coming down may just hit us first. The only thing we've seen was the aeroplanes throwing bombs over Poznan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened subsequently? What happened in the following days?

speaker: interviewee
text: The following day, we-- the planes move further into-- inside Poland. And all we just wait-- been waiting for, whatever, can happen. And we have-- I haven't seen any-- any aeroplanes, because from Poznan, when the war started, I moved with my father and the younger brother, my twin brother, to Warsaw to join the Polish forces. So we were all the time moving forwards whilst the Germans were moving toward Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any encounters with the German troops or German army at that stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we have no encounter. We-- we went right to Warsaw. It took us about four days to drive on a bicycle, to ride on a bicycle to-- to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just tell me again who was with you, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My twin brother, Jerzy, and father. So three of us went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we came to the Polish army. And my father was enrolled. And we were going to be enrolled as well as kitchen assistants, because I have only 15-year-old, to help in the kitchen. But soon the Warsaw capitulated and we had to move backwards to Poznan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you when you moved back to Poznan?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went back to Poznan, we-- we stayed for a few more weeks the German occupied Poland. And then, one night, came a German official who said my parents didn't live in Poznan any longer, they were from eastern Poland. And they-- they asked us to be evacuated. They said to my father that my mother can stay with the children, but the father must move. My mother said, no, we go together with our father, because I cannot support the family on my own. And we moved to where they moved us. That means they moved us first to Chenstochov, where we stayed at a-- well, it was a church community, where they gave us food and supplied us with living accommodation. And that's-- that's about it. Later on, I've tried to move to Hungary with my brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, did you have any encounters with the German army then? Did you see any troops on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no encounter. I had encounter with some soldiers when I was trying to get some milk, which was shortage of. They didn't know allow big queues. And they-- one day, they kicked me and said, you go away from there, and don't stand anymore in the queues to get the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: They kicked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they kicked me on the bottom once, and that's about it. And I moved right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see at all how the German army was treating the civilian population at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see much there, because I didn't stay long after the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we tried to-- my two older brothers moved to south Poland in order to go through the Hungarian border, in order to join the Polish forces. Later on, we received a letter that they passed the border, and they were happy to be in the Polish army. Then I, with my twin brother and older brother Zygmunt, tried to move as well to south Poland. But we weren't very fortunate. We were caught on the border, on the Hungarian border, near the town Bukowsko. And after interrogations--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we-- before we get to that, how were you caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- it was in the evening, about 5 o'clock. Ground, it was thick frost. It was February. And we were moving, going, and then German-- German had their patrols on, and they stopped us, asking where are we going? I try to explain, with my brothers, that we were looking for some-- in order to buy some food in the village, because there was shortage of food. And they didn't accept this and locked us up at the prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their attitude towards you, the patrol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- they were very hostile. And they-- they tried to tell us by beating, tell us you wanted to join the Polish legionaries, [INAUDIBLE] in Hungary. Speak the truth. And I said, we-- but we only went here to look for some food, to bring to Poznan, to where my parents lived, in Krakow. Because they were moved to Krakow later on. But they didn't believe us and locked us up in prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were conditions like in the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, very poor. I couldn't eat at the beginning. The food they gave, it was a slice of bread and very watery soup. First day, I didn't-- didn't manage to eat anything. But the next day, I felt hungry and I tried to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the name of the prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Sanok, the prison was. And-- and they brought us three times for interview from the prison, in order to-- to get from us-- to speak the truth what we were trying to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you interviewed on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Each three-- three of us went, and each was interviewed separately. And every time we were beaten in order to get the confession. We knew that if we confessed that we were trying to join the Polish forces, they would shoot us, because they did so in many other prisons. All those who confessed they wanted to join the police forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they beat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are three-- there were three Gestapo men which had the rubber truncheons and beat us anywhere they could. One day, I-- I-- I fainted. Luckily enough they stopped when I fainted. But that's the-- the only way they did, by beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect did all that have on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't-- didn't have any effect, except I was frightened. I tried to avoid any beating. One day, I remember, from fright, I even made a mess in my trousers. And it smelled so much that the Gestapo stopped beating us, because they couldn't stand the smell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell them what they wanted to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I didn't confess to the crime they wanted from me, to know that I was going to join the Polish forces. And that made a difference, because we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They also kept to the same, which we agreed what we were going to tell them. And none of us broke down.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in this prison for how long, you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They moved us to Tarnow and then to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away is Tarnow from Sanok?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 20 mile-- 20 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why they moved you to Tarnow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they moved us because that's where they moved every transport which they were taking from various prisons in Poland. And there-- from there, they sent further on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you in Tarnow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tarnow, it was just a matter of a month I stayed. there. It was no different. It was more-- more organized prison than Sanok, which was little village. And I've seen prisoners there, but the life was as bearable as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They not where-- there-- I wasn't interviewed, anything. They all just-- all-- I was just waiting for the next stage, which was going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know you were going to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. I-- I had no idea where-- where we were going and what they're going to do with us. But I knew they were going to move us somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: By what they called [INAUDIBLE], big lorries. They put-- chained us up and took us by lorries.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of you were there in the lorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 40 people-- 20 people in one lorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are conditions like on the lorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- in the lorry wasn't-- not so bad. They moved us to train. From the lorries, they moved us to train and loaded us into train, from which we went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were conditions like in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was to short journey, so I didn't feel anything except the change, that's all, which makes a difference, always, in a stable life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long, roughly, was the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long did it-- the journey last? About an hour, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were also taken together with us, with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you reached Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We reached Auschwtiz, where-- where dressed and had our names given and-- and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date did you get to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Auschwitz on the 30th of August, in the summertime, in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: August 30th, 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went into Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Into Auschwitz, where I've been-- tattoo-- a tattoo written.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did you know what it was when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had the-- I seen the prisoners when they stood and looked at the-- the railway line, how we-- how we arrived and so on. But I had no idea. They looked very miserable to me. They had striped clothing on, and it reminded me of prisoners which I've seen on-- on television.

speaker: interviewer
text: What procedures did you undergo when you got to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they opened the train, couple of wagons. And they started shouting, 'Alles out! Alles out!' And they started beating us in order to make sure we moved quickly. And then when we were in the Auschwitz camp, they-- we had seen the Lagerkommandant, [? Hoess, ?] who told us, don't forget you're not coming to a pension-- holiday camp. You coming to do the concentration camp of Auschwitz. And after that, he-- he started asking, are there any priests or Jews? And when a few of them, about 10 of them, came out of there, they took them to the penalty company, which most likely, I doubt they survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what part of Auschwitz camp you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the main camp, Auschwitz camp number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living quarters like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were a big accommodation, big room, which people lied on the floor first, because we had no beds. Later on, they-- so they made some beds, three-- three tier beds, where we-- we slept on a straw mattress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your brothers at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all-- well, I stayed with my twin brother, my older brother stayed in different block, where he-- where he was living separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the routine like in Auschwitz at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, in the morning, you had a gong giving you time 6 o'clock to get up. And the kapos came into the room with sticks. And whoever was slowing getting up, they moved-- beat them up to get ready. Had a quick wash in cold water. And then an appel, where-- a roll up, they call it in English. And a roll up where we were counted and then moved to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, the work was very hard, unloading bricks or digging trenches, which, at the beginning, was not so bad, but later on, I felt, with the amount of food I am consuming, that I have no strength left to carry on with the-- with the speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of food was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had once, coffee in the morning, watery coffee, then soup, beetroot soup, or whatever watery soup they gave us. And then, in the evening, we had a slice of bread. That's-- that's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get on without food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had to consume as much as I can. And later on, I tried to see if I could get anywhere something to-- to compensate for the lack of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the routine change for you, subsequently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a matter of getting better job, because on that kind of job, where I had unloading bricks or digging trenches, I knew I will not survive. And I had an information given that there is a good job going on. I knew the-- the-- what they call Arbeitsdienst, in German, who gave the work. He used to live-- we've been-- he's been in the same prison as I was in Sanok. And he knew me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ask him if I can get-- there is a vacancy at the rabbit feeding company, breeding company. And he told me, well, I know you. I can give you that job with your brother. And that's how I got that job, which was a lot easier, because sometimes I could get some food what the rabbits had. And it was warm on the roof, which made me easier to survive. How long did you continue doing that job with the rabbits?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was doing the job for the first-- well, I-- they moved us from Auschwitz to another kommando outside Auschwitz, where we were living in a Harmeze Schule, special school. And there, we-- we started living all together. But they also opened a farm house there, chicken farm. And there was a fishery farm for-- for crop fish. And that's where all the people were moved into one place. The people from fishing camp, they were moved to another place, where was someone was evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this work area?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Harmensee, that was the village name. And there-- there was a big house, used to be former palace of [? Zwilling ?], [? Zwilling ?] man, he had a big palace, where we lived at the beginning. But later on, in about half a year later, they employed the women from Birkneau to work on the farm, and we were employed as a subsidiary force for the supply of various items to the Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was discipline like in the camp for the prisoners, for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, it was very, very hard. It was-- every time you had to look over your shoulder that nobody will beat you. The worst-- the worst thing was lack of food. I would have survived the beating, but the lack of food made it worse. The German stated that, on this food, you will survive three months unless you get somewhere else food. People who survived were mainly those who tried to manage to steal some food in order to survive. Usually when the kommandos were out for work, they tried to look where they can pinch something, either a potato or beet or carrot and-- and eat it. Or, usually, when the kommandos came back from work, there used to be many people who couldn't survive the day. And the block managers did not report their death to the kitchen officials in the first few days, so that they can collect their food. And if you have friends there, they can always give you a helping hand in food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this affect your health?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was very good at certain times, but later on, I felt very ill. I had scratched my foot on a-- on a very hard shoe, and this turned down into phlegmon, which means in-- I had to go to hospital for treatment. I managed to get an operation done on my leg, in order to remove all the bad blood. And that, I survived and came back to the block.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your weight?

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your weight?

speaker: interviewee
text: My weight was very, very much down. But I still was very healthy and managed to survive whatever punishment I received.

speaker: interviewer
text: What punishments were these that you received?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes, if you-- if you stood for a while in-- to hide yourself from rain, if somebody see you, you-- you receive beating. Or if you tried to rest for a bit, you had to also watch that kapo doesn't come along to give you a beating for it. These are the things which usually worried me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What stands out in your mind as examples of particular cruelty that you saw in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cruelty was there. I've seen-- I've seen the people in the penalty company being beaten. And they had hard work doing-- and less food than we had. And this was the worst part for me, when seeing people being thrown down the floor, pulled out gold teeth, and so on. These were something I had never seen in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll just pause there-- thank you very much-- and change the-- change the tape. Thank you. Very good. You've giving me a very good graphic description.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. I feel a bit awkward.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well it's very-- very-- very-- very--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's different than writing.
